title: practical english usage - Part 81
content:
grammar •  97  infinitives after verbs: It's beginning to rain.

I didn 't ask you to pay for the meal, (     I didn't ask that you-payfor not They don't allow people to smoke, (     They don 't allow dint-people smoke ] not She didn 't want me to go. (     She didn 't want thaET-go?) With some verbs (e.g. want, allow), a thɑ t-clause is impossible.

I want you to listen.

Many verbs are followed by object + infinitive.

to   listen.

98 verb    + object     + infinitive:      I want      you For information about the structures that are possible with a particular verb, see a good dictionary.

For be + infinitive (e.g.  You are to start tomorrow), ►    42.

For perfect infinitives after verbs, ►    90.   For have + infinitive (e.g. I have to go now), ►    74.

I enjoy sailing, (     I enjoy to-saU.) followed by -ing forms (►   100).

After some verbs, it is not possible to use an infinitive. Many of these can be (sometimes with a difference of meaning). For details, ►     105.

After some verbs we  can use not only an infinitive but also an -ing form of these, ►     113.7.

infinitive (e.g. I arranged for her to have violin lessons). For details happy). For details, ►    98. A  few verbs are followed by verb + for + object + Some of these verbs can be followed by object + infinitive (e.g. I want her to be beg    expect  hope    offer   remember (can't) beardecide  hesitate neglect  regret  wish attempt dare   help    mean    refuse   want ask    continue hate    manage  promise  try arrange consent  happen  love    propose trouble appear choose  go on    like    pretend swear agree   care   forget   learn   prepare start afford  begin   fail    intend  prefer  seem some of these, see the Index): Common verbs that can be followed by infinitives (for more detailed entries on The car needs to be cleaned.

I expect to have finished by tomorrow evening.

She seems to be crying.

I don't want to see you again.

It's beginning to rain.

After many non-auxiliary verbs, we  can use the infinitives of other verbs.

97 infinitives       after    verbs:   It's  beginning           to   rairL grammar •  99  try and . .., go and. . ., etc She thought of going and getting him.

She stayed and played with the children.

He often comes and spends the evening with us.

With these verbs, the structure is not only used with the base form.

Come and have a drink. Stay and have dinner. Hurry up and open the door.

often used informally.

Come and... , go and..., run and..., hurry up and..., stay and. .. are 2 come!go,       etc   and    ...

We waited to see what would happen, (     We waited and saw.. .) not I tried to eat something, (     J-tried-and-ate-something.) Try and eat something.

She tries to keep a straight face... (     She triesandrkeeps ...) I try and keep a straight face when he talks, but I can't help smiling.

not possible, for example, with tries, trying, was sure or waited. Compare: We only use this structure with the simple base forms try / be sure / wait. It is 'What's for lunch? 'Wait and see.' Note also the common expression Wait and see.

Be sure and ask Uncle Joe about his garden.

I'll try and phone you tomorrow morning.

Try and eat something - you'll feel better if you do.

We   often use and.. . instead of to after try / be sure. This is informal.

1  try/be      sure     / wait     and    ...

99 try    and    ..., go   and    ... , etc  ______________ choice), ►     10.3 —   10.4.   For structures with take (e.g. The ferry took two hours to unload), ►    602.

For object + to be + complement after verbs of thinking and feeling (e.g. / considered him  to be an excellent For verbs that are followed by for + object + infinitive (e.g. I arranged for her to go early), ►     113.7.

For passive structures with these verbs, ►    64.

an -ing form or a that-clause. For complete information, see a good dictionary.

Many of the verbs listed above can also be followed by other structures such as not I suggested that she should go home, (     /  suggested her to go home.) Some verbs cannot be followed by object + infinitive; for example suggest.
